root_text.an4.164:1.3: Akkhamā paṭipadā, khamā paṭipadā, damā paṭipadā, samā paṭipadā.
root_text.an4.164:1.5: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco akkosantaṁ paccakkosati, rosantaṁ paṭirosati, bhaṇḍantaṁ paṭibhaṇḍati.
root_text.an4.164:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco akkosantaṁ na paccakkosati, rosantaṁ na paṭirosati, bhaṇḍantaṁ na paṭibhaṇḍati.
root_text.an4.164:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā na nimittaggāhī hoti nānubyañjanaggāhī;
root_text.an4.164:3.3: yatvādhikaraṇamenaṁ cakkhundriyaṁ asaṁvutaṁ viharantaṁ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṁ, tassa saṁvarāya paṭipajjati; rakkhati cakkhundriyaṁ; cakkhundriye saṁvaraṁ āpajjati.
root_text.an4.164:3.8: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya na nimittaggāhī hoti nānubyañjanaggāhī;
root_text.an4.164:3.9: yatvādhikaraṇamenaṁ manindriyaṁ asaṁvutaṁ viharantaṁ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṁ, tassa saṁvarāya paṭipajjati; rakkhati manindriyaṁ; manindriye saṁvaraṁ āpajjati.
root_text.an4.164:4.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu uppannaṁ kāmavitakkaṁ nādhivāseti pajahati vinodeti sameti byantīkaroti anabhāvaṁ gameti; uppannaṁ byāpādavitakkaṁ …pe… uppannaṁ vihiṁsāvitakkaṁ … uppannuppanne pāpake akusale dhamme nādhivāseti pajahati vinodeti sameti byantīkaroti anabhāvaṁ gameti.

translation_text.an4.164:1.1: “Mendicants, there are four ways of practice.
translation_text.an4.164:1.3: Impatient practice, patient practice, taming practice, and calming practice.
translation_text.an4.164:1.5: It’s when someone abuses, annoys, or argues with you, and you abuse, annoy, or argue right back at them.
translation_text.an4.164:1.6: This is called the impatient practice.
translation_text.an4.164:2.2: It’s when someone abuses, annoys, or argues with you, and you don’t abuse, annoy, or argue back at them.
translation_text.an4.164:2.3: This is called the patient practice.
translation_text.an4.164:3.2: When a mendicant sees a sight with their eyes, they don’t get caught up in the features and details.
translation_text.an4.164:3.3: If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving restraint over it.
translation_text.an4.164:3.4: When they hear a sound with their ears …
translation_text.an4.164:3.5: When they smell an odor with their nose …
translation_text.an4.164:3.6: When they taste a flavor with their tongue …
translation_text.an4.164:3.7: When they feel a touch with their body …
translation_text.an4.164:3.8: When they know an idea with their mind, they don’t get caught up in the features and details.
translation_text.an4.164:3.9: If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, they practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving restraint over it.
translation_text.an4.164:3.10: This is called the taming practice.
translation_text.an4.164:4.2: It’s when a mendicant doesn’t tolerate a sensual, malicious, or cruel thought. They don’t tolerate any bad, unskillful qualities that have arisen, but give them up, get rid of them, calm them, eliminate them, and obliterate them.
translation_text.an4.164:4.3: This is called the calming practice.
translation_text.an4.164:4.4: These are the four ways of practice.”
